title: Stacks & Queues
description: Stacks and queues appear in nearly every FAANG interview — often as the hidden backbone of a 'hard' problem. The monotonic stack and queue-based BFS patterns are must-knows.

sections.title: Stack & Queue Implementations
sections.description: Python list as stack: O(1) amortized append/pop from right. collections.deque as queue: O(1) append/popleft. Never use list as a queue — list.pop(0) is O(N) due to shifting.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import deque

# Stack (LIFO) — use list
stack = []
stack.append(1)   # push O(1)
stack.append(2)
stack.append(3)
print(stack.pop())  # 3, O(1)
print(stack[-1])    # peek without pop

# Queue (FIFO) — use deque, NOT list
queue = deque()
queue.append(1)        # enqueue O(1)
queue.append(2)
print(queue.popleft()) # dequeue O(1)

# Deque as both stack and queue
dq = deque()
dq.append(1)       # right push O(1)
dq.appendleft(0)   # left push O(1)
dq.pop()           # right pop O(1)
dq.popleft()       # left pop O(1)

# Priority Queue (min-heap)
import heapq
pq = []
heapq.heappush(pq, (1, 'high priority'))
heapq.heappush(pq, (3, 'low priority'))
print(heapq.heappop(pq))  # (1, 'high priority')

sections.title: Min Stack — O(1) getMin
sections.description: Design a stack that supports push, pop, and getMin in O(1). The trick: maintain a parallel auxiliary stack that tracks the current minimum. When you pop, also pop from the auxiliary stack. This avoids O(N) scanning for the new minimum.

sections.title: Monotonic Stack — Next Greater / Smaller Element
sections.description:
Monotonic stacks maintain elements in sorted order. When an element violates the order, we pop — and each pop answers a query. Overall O(N) since each element is pushed and popped at most once.

Pattern: 'Next greater/smaller element', 'Largest rectangle in histogram', 'Daily temperatures', 'Trapping rain water', 'Stock span'.
sections.codeBlock:
# Next Greater Element — O(N)
def next_greater(nums):
    result = [-1] * len(nums)
    stack = []  # monotonic decreasing (indices)
    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
        # Pop all elements smaller than current — current is their next greater
        while stack and nums[stack[-1]] < num:
            result[stack.pop()] = num
        stack.append(i)
    # Remaining in stack have no next greater → result stays -1
    return result

# Daily Temperatures (variant)
def daily_temperatures(temps):
    result = [0] * len(temps)
    stack = []  # indices
    for i, t in enumerate(temps):
        while stack and temps[stack[-1]] < t:
            j = stack.pop()
            result[j] = i - j   # days to wait
        stack.append(i)
    return result

# Largest Rectangle in Histogram — O(N)
def largest_rectangle(heights):
    heights.append(0)  # sentinel to flush remaining stack
    stack = [-1]       # sentinel index
    max_area = 0
    for i, h in enumerate(heights):
        while stack[-1] != -1 and heights[stack[-1]] >= h:
            height = heights[stack.pop()]
            width  = i - stack[-1] - 1
            max_area = max(max_area, height * width)
        stack.append(i)
    return max_area

sections.title: Queue Using Two Stacks
sections.description: Implement a queue using only two stacks. Amortized O(1) per operation — each element is moved from stack1 to stack2 at most once. A classic interview question testing amortized analysis reasoning.

sections.title: Sliding Window Maximum — Monotonic Deque
sections.description: Find the maximum of every sliding window of size K. Brute force is O(NK). Monotonic deque achieves O(N) by keeping a decreasing deque of indices — the front is always the window maximum.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import deque

def max_sliding_window(nums, k):
    """
    Deque stores indices in decreasing order of nums[i].
    Front = index of current window maximum.
    """
    dq = deque()  # stores indices, front = max index
    result = []

    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
        # Remove indices outside window
        while dq and dq[0] < i - k + 1:
            dq.popleft()

        # Remove smaller elements from back (they'll never be the max)
        while dq and nums[dq[-1]] < num:
            dq.pop()

        dq.append(i)

        # Window is full — record maximum (front of deque)
        if i >= k - 1:
            result.append(nums[dq[0]])

    return result

# Example
print(max_sliding_window([1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], k=3))
# [3, 3, 5, 5, 6, 7]

details:
Monotonic Stack/Queue Recognition Pattern: If the brute force involves an inner loop scanning left or right to find the next/previous smaller/larger element, a monotonic stack turns it O(N). If it involves scanning a window for min/max, a monotonic deque turns it O(N). These are table-stakes optimizations at FAANG L5+.

Stack for Parsing: Stacks are natural for balanced brackets, expression evaluation, and recursive structure parsing. The call stack itself is a stack — iterative DFS uses an explicit one. Any time you need 'match this opening thing with its closing thing', think stack.

Deque as Two-Ended Sliding Window: The sliding window maximum deque pattern generalizes. BFS uses deque naturally. The double-ended property enables sliding window problems where you need O(1) operations on both ends simultaneously.
